planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost danger in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil; Indian Coral Tree is a fast-growing deciduous tropical tree native to coastal forests and tidal zones of South Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Western Pacific, producing spectacular clusters of brilliant red-orange pea-family flowers on bare branches in winter and early spring before the boldly variegated trifoliate leaves emerge.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile to moderately fertile soil; Erythrina variegata is remarkably drought tolerant once established — deep, infrequent watering promotes deep root development, and established trees require little supplemental irrigation in subtropical conditions. Erythrina variegata (Indian Coral Tree, Tiger's Claw, or Variegated Coral Tree) is a large, fast-growing deciduous tree native to tropical coastal forests, mangrove edges, and tidal shorelines from the Indian subcontinent through Southeast Asia to the western Pacific islands, where its brilliant scarlet-to-orange, narrowly cylindrical pea-family flowers appear in dense terminal racemes on bare, thorny branches in the dry season before leaf-out — creating one of the most dramatic floral displays of any large tropical tree. The species epithet 'variegata' references a widely grown ornamental cultivar with bold yellow leaf variegation. The soft, lightweight, easily carved wood has been used across South and Southeast Asia for traditional canoe outriggers, floats, and knife handles, and the tree is deeply woven into Balinese Hindu ritual culture where it appears in temple gardens and ceremonial use. In Southeast Asian countries it is widely planted as a street tree and temple tree, blooming spectacularly in the dry season. A nitrogen-fixer in the legume family, it enriches soils around it. Excellent large specimen tree for tropical and warm subtropical gardens.
sources: PIER Pacific Island Ecosystems at Risk — Erythrina variegata
Bali Hindu Temple Trees — Royal Botanic Gardens Kew

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Sea Mango is a striking tropical coastal tree with fragrant white and pink flowers and large red ornamental fruits — ALL parts of the plant, especially the seeds, are extremely toxic and contain cerberin, a powerful cardiac glycoside — handle with caution and plant away from children and livestock.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Cerbera manghas tolerates brief coastal flooding, salt spray, and periodic waterlogging in its native coastal forest habitat — water regularly in establishment, then moderately once established. Cerbera manghas (Sea Mango or Pong-pong) is an evergreen tropical tree native to the coastal forests, mangrove margins, and beach strands from Madagascar and the East African coast through South Asia, Southeast Asia, and into the Pacific islands, where it is one of the quintessential components of coastal tropical forest. The tree produces clusters of large, intensely fragrant white flowers with a bright pink or red center tube, ornamentally comparable to frangipani, followed by large (3–4 inch), smooth, shiny, green-then-red ornamental fruits resembling a small mango — entirely toxic and not to be eaten. All parts of Cerbera manghas contain cerberin, neriifolin, and related cardiac glycosides among the most toxic plant compounds known; historically the seeds were used in trial-by-ordeal poison rites in Madagascar and across the Pacific. Despite its toxicity, Sea Mango is widely planted as an ornamental street and coastal tree throughout tropical Asia and the Pacific for its compact, attractive form, fragrant flowers, and colorful fruits; it is tolerant of salt, wind, and coastal conditions. Plant in locations inaccessible to children and livestock and always wash hands after handling.
sources: Flora of Southeast Asia — Cerbera manghas
Poison Plant Database — University of Cornell
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Cerbera manghas

planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost danger in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–12; Tropical Hydrangea is a fast-growing East African shrub or small tree that produces spectacular, large, drooping globular clusters of pink to deep rose flowers in winter and spring — resembling oversized hydrangea flower heads on a tropical tree — and is one of the most ornamental flowering trees for Gulf Coast and tropical gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Dombeya wallichii is fast growing and responds well to pruning after flowering to maintain a compact form — cut back one-third of the plant after each bloom cycle to encourage bushiness and the next flush of blooms. Dombeya wallichii (Tropical Hydrangea or Pinkball Tree) is a large, fast-growing evergreen to semi-deciduous shrub or small tree native to Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, and the East African highlands, where it grows at elevations of 3,000–7,000 feet in forest edges, clearings, and disturbed habitats. The large, pendulous, globular clusters of pink to deep rose, 5-petaled flowers closely resemble oversized hydrangea flower heads and appear dramatically in winter and early spring, often nearly obscuring the large, velvety, heart-shaped leaves. The dried, spent flower clusters remain attractively on the plant for weeks after petal fall, persisting as papery, straw-colored globes. Dombeya is a consistent performer in zones 9–12, flowering reliably in winter when few other large flowering shrubs are in bloom — making it exceptionally valuable for winter interest in Gulf Coast, Florida, and Southern California gardens. Mulch well, maintain consistent moisture, and fertilize monthly during the growing season for best flowering. Plants freeze to the ground in zone 9a frost events but typically regrow rapidly from the root crown.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dombeya wallichii

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist to dry, well-drained, acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; St. Andrews Cross is a small, adaptable native shrub of the southeastern United States bearing distinctive cross-shaped, 4-petaled yellow flowers in summer — the only North American St. John's Wort with only 4 petals — and is exceptionally shade and drought tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, acidic, sandy to loamy, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Hypericum hypericoides establishes readily in poor, infertile, acidic soils and is excellent for shaded edges, pine understory plantings, and woodland gardens — requires no supplemental fertilizer or irrigation once established in appropriate soil. Hypericum hypericoides (St. Andrews Cross) is a small, multi-stemmed, deciduous native shrub of the eastern and southeastern United States, ranging from New England south through Florida, west through Oklahoma and Texas, and into the Caribbean, where it grows in dry to moist pine flatwoods, sandhills, woodland edges, and stream banks. The common name and species epithet both reference the unusual cross-like arrangement of the 4 petals — unique among North American Hypericum species, all others of which have 5 petals — resembling the X-shaped cross of St. Andrew (the Saltire). Small, bright-yellow flowers appear prolifically in summer, followed by small, 2-valved capsules. A remarkably adaptable native shrub that performs well in both dry and moist conditions, acid soils, sandy substrates, full sun and deep partial shade — one of the most useful native shrubs for low-maintenance naturalistic planting in the Southeast. Valuable for pollinators, particularly native bees that collect pollen from the numerous stamens.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society — Hypericum hypericoides
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Hypericum hypericoides

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile to average soil; Coast Coral Tree is a spectacular South African native and one of the most widely planted street trees of Southern California, capable of brief light frosts in zone 8–9, and produces masses of brilliant red-orange flowers on bare thorny branches in late winter and spring before the feathery trifoliate leaves emerge.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained soil; Erythrina caffra is drought tolerant once established — water deeply and infrequently during summer in Mediterranean and subtropical climates and rely on winter rainfall in coastal California conditions; avoid heavy clay with poor drainage. Erythrina caffra (Coast Coral Tree or South African Coral Tree) is a large, semi-deciduous tree native to the coastal forests and river margins of Eastern South Africa (KwaZulu-Natal, Eastern Cape) that has become one of the defining ornamental trees of the Southern California street landscape, the official city tree of Los Angeles. Trees lose most of their leaves in late winter and simultaneously burst into spectacular bloom — the dense, columnar racemes of bright coral-red to orange pea-family flowers covering the bare, spiny branches from January through March create an unforgettable winter floral display unmatched by almost any other tree in temperate gardens. The flowers are rich in nectar and attract hummingbirds, lorikeets, and sunbirds across its global planted range. Erythrina caffra is more cold-hardy than the related E. variegata — tolerating brief periods near 25°F when established in well-drained soil — which has made it the standard large coral tree for zone 8b–9 California and Southeastern gardens. A nitrogen-fixer that enriches surrounding soil; useful for large spaces, street medians, and parks.
sources: Los Angeles City Tree — Erythrina caffra
University of California Cooperative Extension — Coral Trees in California

subcategory: Tropical American food and timber tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile soil; West Indian Locust is a large, long-lived tropical timber and food tree of the Caribbean and tropical Americas bearing hard, woody pods enclosing a dry, starchy, edible flour-like pulp with a distinctive sweet-sour flavor — highly nutritious and an important traditional food source — and producing the hard, amber-colored Jutahy copal resin used for varnishes and incense.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, fertile to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Hymenaea courbaril is drought tolerant and adapted to seasonal dry periods once established — water regularly in establishment years, then rely primarily on rainfall in tropical conditions. Hymenaea courbaril (West Indian Locust, Jatobá, or South American Locust) is a massive, long-lived, evergreen to semi-deciduous tree native to the Caribbean islands, Central America, and tropical South America from Mexico through the Amazon Basin, where it is one of the dominant canopy trees of seasonally dry and humid tropical forests. The tree produces large, hard, oblong, woody, dark-brown pods enclosing a dry, starchy, sweet-sour, floury pulp around the seeds — an important traditional food of indigenous peoples throughout its range, eaten directly, ground into flour for bread and porridge, or fermented into beverages. The pulp is nutritionally dense, rich in carbohydrates, protein, and calcium. Hymenaea courbaril is also one of the primary historical sources of Jutahy (or jatobá) copal resin, exuded from the trunk and roots as a hard amber-colored substance used in traditional varnishes, incense, and folk medicine for respiratory conditions and skin ailments. The extremely dense, hard, durable heartwood — among the hardest commercial timbers of the Americas — has been used for heavy construction, flooring, furniture, and tool handles for centuries. A very slow-growing, exceptionally long-lived tree requiring 10+ years to first fruit.
sources: CABI Invasive Species Compendium — Hymenaea courbaril
CIFOR Forests and Food Security — Jatobá
